description: A minimal, ultra-fast zsh syntax highlighter written in Rust with a thin zsh plugin. Zero configuration — install with brew and forget. Highlights commands (valid/invalid), reserved words, strings, comments, and operators using terminal palette colors.

userStories.title: Project scaffolding and binary skeleton
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the Rust project structure set up with a working binary that reads argv[1] and $_ZH_CMDS env var, so that subsequent stories can build on a compiling foundation.
acceptanceCriteria: Cargo.toml exists with package name 'zsh-highlighter', edition 2021, no external dependencies
Cargo.toml has release profile: opt-level=3, lto=true, codegen-units=1, panic='abort', strip=true
src/main.rs reads argv[1] as the input buffer (exits silently with code 0 if missing or empty)
src/main.rs reads $_ZH_CMDS env var and splits on newlines into a HashSet (proceeds with empty set if missing)
src/tokenizer.rs exists with a public Token struct: start: usize, end: usize, token_type: TokenType
src/tokenizer.rs has TokenType enum with variants: Word { command_position: bool }, String, Comment, Operator, ReservedWord
src/tokenizer.rs has a public fn tokenize(input: &str) -> Vec<Token> that returns empty Vec for now
src/classifier.rs exists with a public Highlight struct: start: usize, end: usize, style: &'static str
src/classifier.rs has a public fn classify(tokens: &[Token], known_commands: &HashSet<&str>) -> Vec<Highlight> that returns empty Vec for now
main.rs calls tokenize, then classify, then prints each Highlight as '{start} {end} {style}' to stdout (one per line)
cargo build --release compiles with zero warnings
cargo clippy passes with no warnings
Running the binary with no args exits with code 0 and produces no output
Running the binary with an empty string arg exits with code 0 and produces no output

userStories.title: Complete tokenizer: words, strings, comments, operators
userStories.description: As a developer, I want a complete single-pass tokenizer that correctly splits zsh input into typed tokens (words, strings, comments, operators, backticks, line continuations), so that downstream classification has accurate token boundaries.
acceptanceCriteria: The tokenizer is implemented as a single-pass character-by-character state machine that iterates over the input bytes once

WORD SPLITTING: input is split into tokens on whitespace boundaries. 'ls -la /tmp' produces 3 Word tokens at byte offsets (0,2), (3,6), (7,11). Leading/trailing whitespace is skipped. Empty input returns empty Vec.

SINGLE-QUOTED STRINGS: a single quote starts a String token that ends at the next single quote. No escape processing inside. 'echo '"'"'hello world'"'"'' returns Word(0,4) and String(5,18). Unterminated strings extend to end of input.

DOUBLE-QUOTED STRINGS: a double quote starts a String token. Backslash-doublequote inside is an escape (not end of string). 'echo "hello"' returns Word(0,4) and String(5,12). Unterminated strings extend to end of input.
ANSI-C STRINGS: dollar sign immediately followed by single quote ($') starts a String token. Backslash-singlequote inside is an escape. Unterminated strings extend to end of input.
BACKTICK STRINGS: a backtick starts a String token that ends at the next backtick. 'echo `uname`' returns Word(0,4) and String(5,12). Unterminated backticks extend to end of input.

COMMENTS: a '#' character that is preceded by whitespace (or is at position 0) and is NOT inside any string starts a Comment token that extends to the end of the current line (or end of input). '#' in the middle of a word (e.g., 'foo#bar') is NOT a comment.
OPERATORS: the following characters/sequences break tokens and produce Operator tokens: | || |& && ; ;; > >> < << <<< & ( )
OPERATOR GREEDINESS: multi-character operators are matched greedily — || before |, >> before >, << before <, ;; before ;, <<< before <<
BRACES AS OPERATORS: { and } produce Operator tokens (they break words like other operators). They will be restyled as reserved words by the classifier later.
DOUBLE BRACKETS: [[ and ]] produce Operator tokens when the two bracket characters appear consecutively. A single [ or ] is part of a regular word.
LINE CONTINUATIONS: backslash immediately followed by newline (\n) is treated as whitespace (skipped). It does NOT appear in any token.
ADJACENCY: tokens can be adjacent without whitespace when an operator or string boundary separates them. 'echo>file' produces Word(0,4), Operator(4,5), Word(5,9).
All token offsets are byte offsets (not char offsets) to match zsh region_highlight format

Unit tests exist covering: basic word splitting, each string type (normal + unterminated), comments (standalone, inline, inside strings NOT treated as comments, after operators), each operator, operator greediness, braces, double brackets, line continuations, adjacent tokens, empty input, whitespace-only input
cargo test passes
cargo clippy passes with no warnings
userStories.notes: This is the largest story. Build the tokenizer as a single-pass state machine with states like: Default, InWord, InSingleQuote, InDoubleQuote, InAnsiCQuote, InBacktick, InComment. All Word tokens should have command_position: false for now — command position is handled in US-003.

userStories.title: Command position detection and reserved words
userStories.description: As a developer, I want each Word token tagged with whether it's in command position, and reserved words identified as ReservedWord tokens, so that the classifier can determine valid/invalid commands.
acceptanceCriteria: The first Word token in the input is in command position: 'git status' — 'git' has command_position=true, 'status' has command_position=false
A Word after pipe | is in command position: 'ls | grep foo' — both 'ls' and 'grep' are command position
A Word after || is in command position: 'false || echo hi' — 'false' and 'echo' are command position
A Word after && is in command position: 'true && ls' — 'true' and 'ls' are command position
A Word after ; or ;; is in command position: 'cd /tmp; ls' — 'cd' and 'ls' are command position
A Word after ( is in command position: '(echo hi)' — 'echo' is command position
A Word after { operator is in command position: '{ echo hi; }' — 'echo' is command position

A Word in command position that matches a reserved word becomes a ReservedWord token instead of a Word token. Reserved words are: if then else elif fi for in while until do done case esac function select repeat time
After these reserved words, the next Word is in command position: then, else, elif, do, time, and ! (negation)
After 'for', 'case', 'select', the next Word is NOT in command position (it's a variable name or match expression)
After 'in', the next Word is NOT in command position (it's a list element)
The ! token (when in command position) becomes a ReservedWord and the next Word is in command position
'if true; then echo hi; fi' produces: ReservedWord('if'), Word('true' cmd=true), Operator(';'), ReservedWord('then'), Word('echo' cmd=true), Word('hi' cmd=false), Operator(';'), ReservedWord('fi')
Second and subsequent words in a simple command are NOT in command position
Operator tokens { and } are restyled: their token_type changes from Operator to ReservedWord by the command position pass
Operator tokens [[ and ]] are restyled: their token_type changes from Operator to ReservedWord by the command position pass
After [[ operator/reserved word, words are NOT in command position (they are conditional arguments)
Unit tests exist covering all above patterns
cargo test passes
cargo clippy passes with no warnings
userStories.notes: Implement as a second pass over the token list from US-002. Walk the tokens, track whether the next word should be in command position using a boolean flag. When a Word is in command position, check if it matches the reserved words list — if so, change its TokenType to ReservedWord.

userStories.title: Classifier: map tokens to region_highlight styles
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the classifier to map token types to zsh region_highlight style strings, including command validation against $_ZH_CMDS, so that the binary produces correct output.
acceptanceCriteria: Word with command_position=true found in known_commands set maps to 'fg=green,bold'
Word with command_position=true NOT found in known_commands set maps to 'fg=red,underline'
Word with command_position=false produces NO highlight (arguments are unstyled)
ReservedWord tokens map to 'fg=yellow,bold'
String tokens map to 'fg=yellow'
Comment tokens map to 'fg=8'
Operator tokens map to 'fg=cyan'
When known_commands HashSet is empty, all command-position words map to 'fg=green,bold' (safe default — per SPEC)
End-to-end: input='ls -la /tmp' with known_commands={'ls'} produces only '0 2 fg=green,bold' (ls is valid command, -la and /tmp are unstyled arguments)
End-to-end: input='gti status' with known_commands={'git','ls'} produces '0 3 fg=red,underline' (gti is unknown)
End-to-end: input='if true; then echo hi; fi' with known_commands={'true','echo'} produces highlights for all reserved words (fg=yellow,bold), valid commands (fg=green,bold), and operators (fg=cyan)
End-to-end: input='echo "hello world"' with known_commands={'echo'} produces '0 4 fg=green,bold' and the string range with 'fg=yellow'
End-to-end: input='# a comment' produces one highlight spanning the full input with 'fg=8'
Unit tests for classify covering each token type to style mapping
cargo test passes
cargo clippy passes with no warnings

userStories.title: Zsh plugin: core highlighting hook
userStories.description: As a user, I want a zsh plugin that hooks into the line editor and calls the binary on each buffer change to apply syntax highlighting.
acceptanceCriteria: plugin/zsh-highlighter.zsh exists and is under 50 lines
Plugin locates the binary: checks ${0:A:h}/../bin/zsh-highlighter first, then falls back to command -v zsh-highlighter in PATH
Plugin builds $_ZH_CMDS from ${(k)commands} ${(k)builtins} ${(k)aliases} ${(k)functions} joined with newlines and exports it
Plugin uses 'autoload -Uz add-zle-hook-widget' and registers _zh_highlight via 'add-zle-hook-widget line-pre-redraw _zh_highlight'
_zh_highlight function returns immediately if BUFFER is empty
_zh_highlight returns immediately if BUFFER equals $_zh_prev_buffer (unchanged since last call)
_zh_highlight returns immediately if ${#BUFFER} > 10000 (10KB safety cap)
_zh_highlight stores _zh_prev_buffer=$BUFFER before calling binary
_zh_highlight calls the binary with $BUFFER as its argument and captures stdout into a local variable
_zh_highlight sets region_highlight=() then populates it from the binary output lines
If binary is not found at plugin load time, plugin returns silently — no errors, no highlighting
If binary returns non-zero exit code, region_highlight is set to empty (clear any previous highlights)
Plugin does NOT source other files or require external dependencies
zsh -n plugin/zsh-highlighter.zsh passes syntax check (run with: zsh -n plugin/zsh-highlighter.zsh)
userStories.notes: This is the user-facing integration point. Must handle all failure modes gracefully and silently.

userStories.title: Zsh plugin: zh-reload and chpwd hook
userStories.description: As a user, I want a zh-reload command to refresh the command cache after installing new tools, and automatic cache refresh on directory change.
acceptanceCriteria: A _zh_rebuild_cmds function exists that builds and exports $_ZH_CMDS (same logic as initial load, extracted to avoid duplication)
Plugin init calls _zh_rebuild_cmds instead of inline $_ZH_CMDS construction
zh-reload function is defined and available to the user
zh-reload calls 'rehash' (updates zsh command hash table)
zh-reload calls _zh_rebuild_cmds
zh-reload unsets _zh_prev_buffer to force re-highlight on next keystroke
zh-reload prints: 'zsh-highlighter: command cache reloaded'
chpwd hook is registered via 'autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook' and 'add-zsh-hook chpwd _zh_chpwd'
_zh_chpwd calls _zh_rebuild_cmds and unsets _zh_prev_buffer
Plugin is still under 50 lines total
zsh -n plugin/zsh-highlighter.zsh passes syntax check

userStories.title: Integration tests for common shell patterns
userStories.description: As a developer, I want comprehensive integration tests verifying the full tokenize+classify pipeline produces correct region_highlight output for real-world shell commands.
acceptanceCriteria: Tests exist in tests/integration.rs that call the public tokenize and classify functions
Helper function: takes input string + known commands list, runs tokenize then classify, returns Vec<Highlight>
Test: 'ls -la /tmp' with ls known — ls gets fg=green,bold; -la and /tmp get no highlight
Test: 'gti status' with git known — gti gets fg=red,underline
Test: 'echo "hello world"' with echo known — echo gets fg=green,bold, the quoted string gets fg=yellow
Test: 'cat file.txt | grep pattern' with cat and grep known — both commands fg=green,bold, pipe fg=cyan
Test: 'if true; then echo ok; fi' with true and echo known — if/then/fi fg=yellow,bold, true/echo fg=green,bold, semicolons fg=cyan
Test: '# this is a comment' — entire input fg=8
Test: 'echo hello # inline comment' with echo known — echo fg=green,bold, hello unstyled, '# inline comment' fg=8
Test: empty string — no highlights
Test: 'for i in 1 2 3; do echo $i; done' with echo known — for/in/do/done fg=yellow,bold, echo fg=green,bold
Test: input with $'...' ANSI-C string — string portion gets fg=yellow
Test: 'echo hello > file' with echo known — redirect > gets fg=cyan
Test: '{ echo hi; }' with echo known — braces fg=yellow,bold (reserved word), echo fg=green,bold, semicolons fg=cyan
Test: '[[ -f file ]]' — [[ and ]] get fg=yellow,bold
Test: 'true && false || echo done' with all known — all three commands fg=green,bold, && and || fg=cyan
All tests pass with cargo test
cargo clippy passes with no warnings

userStories.title: Homebrew formula, README, and LICENSE
userStories.description: As a user, I want to install zsh-highlighter via Homebrew with a single command and have clear setup instructions.
acceptanceCriteria: Formula/zsh-highlighter.rb exists with valid Homebrew formula structure
Formula specifies: desc, homepage (GitHub repo URL), license 'MIT', depends_on 'rust' => :build
Formula install method: runs 'system cargo build --release', installs binary from target/release/ to bin/, installs plugin/zsh-highlighter.zsh to share/zsh-highlighter/
Formula has a caveats block instructing users to add: source "$(brew --prefix)/share/zsh-highlighter/zsh-highlighter.zsh" to ~/.zshrc
README.md exists with: project name, one-line description, Installation section (brew + .zshrc line), Usage section (what it highlights, zh-reload), License section
README is concise — under 80 lines total
LICENSE file exists with MIT license text, copyright year 2026, copyright holder 'zsh-highlighter contributors'
cargo build --release compiles successfully
cargo clippy passes with no warnings
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: Final story. After this, the project is shippable.
